market/ai-thesis/index.html@c4b8a8d9c7cf.overall: This is a confident, high-specificity, strongly voiced prose essay with a clear and defensible claim — but it is not yet an interactive explainer. The structural failure is the complete absence of widgets: the article references two charts that don't exist in the HTML, and without them the argument rests entirely on prose in a format whose entire mission is proving things through interactivity. The secondary weakness is scope creep — the 9-section length pulls the article toward investment manifesto territory, which risks diluting the one-thing into a portfolio construction guide. Fix the widg

verdicts.evidence: The hero subtitle states it directly: 'A geopolitical survival race funded by free cash flow, not speculation.' Section I's blockquote reinforces within the first 200 words: 'There is no AI bubble. This is a race for survival. When people and countries get desperate, there is no end to spending.'

There are no interactive widgets anywhere in the HTML. The article twice references visuals that don't exist — 'Look at the chart above' (Section II) and 'Look at that chart' (Section VIII) — but no canvas, SVG, or chart elements appear in the source. The argument is carried entirely by prose.
verdicts.suggestion: At minimum, build two widgets: (1) a side-by-side capex-funding-source comparison (dotcom era: external capital vs. hyperscaler era: FCF) to prove the structural difference in Section II, and (2) a Meta stock price vs. revenue overlay for Section VIII to prove the drawdown-vs-fundamentals disconnect

verdicts.evidence: The hero subtitle lands a genuinely surprising data claim: 'Strip AI out of US GDP and the number turns negative.' This is specific, falsifiable, and counter to the casual 'AI bubble' narrative most readers arrive with.

No widgets exist to evaluate. The article references two absent charts; if those charts were present, the concern would be whether each is scoped to a single concept. Sections IV and VII each carry 4–6 sub-arguments in prose that would each warrant a distinct widget.
verdicts.suggestion: When widgets are added, enforce one concept per widget strictly: the FCF-vs-speculative-funding comparison is one widget; the value chain layer diagram is another; the aggregation buying system is a third. Do not bundle them.

verdicts.evidence: Voice is exceptionally strong throughout. 'I have roughly 80% of my entire stock portfolio in AI-related positions. I have close to $300,000 USD riding on Meta alone. I am not writing this from the sidelines offering opinions. I am backing every word with real money.' The 'you' is addressed directly

Two well-placed sidenotes: 'Well, actually 🧐 — what could make this thesis wrong' (Section II, addressing revenue-per-capex compression if model efficiency gains outpace demand), and 'Well, actually 🧐 — circular financing has historical precedent, and it ended badly' (Section III, the Cisco/Lucent/N

The core claim is articulable: 'AI isn't a bubble because the spending is funded by real FCF, not speculation, and geopolitical necessity means it can't stop.' But the article spans 9 sections covering a value chain taxonomy, a 7-step portfolio construction system, and a drawdown management playbook
verdicts.suggestion: The final synthesis section (IX) should close with the two-sentence teachable version of the claim explicitly — not a 7-point action framework. Something like: 'The AI supercycle isn't a bubble because the spending is funded by the largest free cash flow machines in history, not by external capital

market/capital-rotation/index.html@549ca.overall: A strong, opinionated playbook with excellent specificity, genuine first-person voice, and well-placed sidenotes that honestly flag the model's limits — the quality fundamentals are solid. The main gap is M8: the article teaches the rotation system thoroughly across nine sections but never consolidates the framework dimensions into a single teachable summary, which means a non-expert finishes knowing the core loop but would struggle to name the full playbook structure back. One summary callout box in Section II would close that gap and make the article shippable without other changes.

verdicts.evidence: The hero subtitle states it before any prose: 'Buy beaten-down sectors, hold through recovery, sell at resistance, repeat. The capital-rotation playbook that turns a flat Nifty into a positive return.' The framework name and purpose are unambiguous on the first screen.

market/global-portfolio/index.html@a9ae8.overall: A confident, well-voiced, and empirically specific framework article that delivers on its core currency and compounding argument — the data is honest, the voice is authentic, and the two sidenotes earn real trust. verdicts.evidence: Hero subtitle explicitly names all five dimensions before the body begins: 'This is the five-part playbook for fixing that — currency math, US compounders, the 50% rule, tranche sizing, the global-monopoly thesis.' Well within the first 200 words a reader encounters.

The article contains zero interactive canvas widgets. The only quasi-widget is the math block in Section II (the Return_USD formula), which earns its place. A 24-minute explainer with no interactive elements does not use the format's core capability.

market/investor-psychology/index.html@aa.overall: This is a high-craft Mode B article with strong voice, exceptional specificity, and three widgets that mostly earn their place. The primary structural gap is that the framework — the Behavioural OS — is not named or previewed until Chapter IX; for nine chapters the reader is learning about failure modes without a promised destination, which undercuts the Mode B payoff. Widget 3 also survives on the reader's goodwill rather than the delete test. Fix M1 (name the OS early), sharpen Widget 3 into an argument rather than a diagnostic tool, and make the framework name stick in M8 — this is close to

verdicts.evidence: The six failure modes are listed by name in the third paragraph of Section I ('Loss aversion, panic, FOMO, daily tracking, action bias, anchoring'), well within 200 words. But the framework that countervails them — the 'Behavioural OS' with its pre-trade / hold / post-trade structure — is not named
verdicts.suggestion: Add one sentence to the Section I opening that names the OS and its three-layer structure: e.g. 'This article maps all six, then builds a pre-trade / hold / post-trade operating system that makes them irrelevant.' That preview sets the Mode B frame from the start.

verdicts.evidence: Widgets 1 (utility curve) and 2 (cascade simulator) pass cleanly — removing either would hollow out the core argument. Widget 3 (bias radar) is a self-assessment tool: the slider values map to six pre-labelled sliders and a radar shape, but the argument for why each bias costs money is carried entir
verdicts.suggestion: Give Widget 3 a reveal the prose cannot deliver — e.g. plot each bias against an estimated annual return drag (even a toy model), so the radar shows not just 'you have high anchoring' but 'high anchoring at this score costs ~X% per year'. That makes the widget the proof, not the decoration.

verdicts.evidence: The hero subtitle ('90% of traders lose money… because their wetware fires before their software runs') is a good frame, but the 90% statistic is now widely cited after the SEBI studies and the 'it's psychology not skill' thesis is the dominant retail-investing narrative on every financial platform.
verdicts.suggestion: Lead with the specific, quantifiable asymmetry — '₹1 of loss hurts 2.25× as much as ₹1 of gain feels good, which means your brain is pricing risk wrong by a known, measurable factor' — rather than the population-level 90% stat. The λ number is the genuine surprise; use it as the first screen hook.

verdicts.evidence: Widgets 1 and 2 each teach exactly one concept. Widget 3 covers six distinct cognitive biases in a single canvas — anchoring, confirmation bias, recency bias, sunk-cost fallacy, overconfidence, and FOMO — each with its own slider. The widget teaches none of them deeply; it tallies them.
verdicts.suggestion: Either reduce Widget 3 to the one or two biases that are most consequential and least obvious (overconfidence and anchoring, say), or split it so each bias has its own micro-widget that demonstrates the bias in action rather than asking the reader to self-rate.

verdicts.evidence: Voice is consistent and strong throughout: 'My own hit rate as an investor is about 60%'; 'Every blow-up I have personally watched at close range had the same shape'; 'you white-knuckle the loser hoping for break-even'. The 'you' and 'I' are present in every section.

Every section grounds its claims in specifics: SEBI 2023 and 2024 study figures (89% losers, ₹1.1 lakh average loss); λ = 2.25 with citation; Indian small-cap corrections of 40–50% on named individual names; the Bikhchandani-Hirshleifer-Welch 1992 cascade paper; 286 penalty kicks from Bar-Eli et al.

Three sidenotes with the canonical marker: Section II ('λ varies by person, by mood, and by P&L size'), Section III ('buy the dip only works inside a process'), and Section IV ('herding is not always irrational'). Each genuinely complicates the simple model rather than just hedging.

The closing callout lists all six failure modes and summarises the OS as 'write the thesis, size correctly, look rarely, act seldom, review honestly' — a reasonable 2-sentence candidate. But the framework name 'Behavioural OS' appears only as a section heading in Chapter IX and is never made sticky.
verdicts.suggestion: Coin a sharper name for the framework — e.g. 'the Six-Override OS' or 'the Wetware Patch' — introduce it in Section I alongside the six failure modes, and repeat it at the end of the callout. A friend should be able to say 'it's the [Name]: six biases, one three-step OS' without re-reading.

market/options-insurance/index.html@2fb8.overall: This is a strong Mode A article: the claim lands in the hero, the widgets prove it in sequence, the voice is confident and specific, and four model-break sidenotes show intellectual honesty. The one structural weakness is Widget 3, which packs the entire Greeks taxonomy into a single interactive panel and risks derailing a non-expert reader into pricing mechanics just when the insurance framing has built momentum. Splitting or pruning that widget would tighten the proof-of-claim arc considerably.

verdicts.evidence: Hero subtitle delivers the counter-intuitive claim immediately: 'The investor with a $1M portfolio and no put is taking more risk than the one with — even though only one of them is doing options.' Section I confirms it within ~150 words: 'options are insurance contracts. That is the entire concept.

All six widgets survive the delete test. Widget 1 establishes the four payoff shapes that every subsequent argument depends on. Widget 2 is the core proof of the claim in real dollars. The hero subtitle inverts the reader's prior: most people believe options are the risky instrument, so the claim that the option-free investor is the riskier one is genuinely counter-intuitive. The inversion is the surprise, and it lands before the first word of body copy.

Widget 3 (Black-Scholes pricer) displays eight simultaneous outputs — price, delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho, intrinsic value, and time value — while also rendering a spot-vs-price curve. That is not one concept; it is the entire Greeks taxonomy in a single panel.
verdicts.suggestion: Split Widget 3 into two panels: one that shows only price across spot for different expiries (proving time-value visually), and a separate, simpler Greek readout introduced when each Greek is discussed. Or collapse the Greek grid to delta + theta only — the two Greeks most relevant to the insurance

verdicts.evidence: First-person is present and specific throughout: 'I had a $1 million US portfolio sitting on 35% paper profits'; 'When markets did fall — tariff anxiety, DeepSeek, rate uncertainty — the insured investor is not panicking.' The voice is opinionated, not encyclopedic.

Concrete figures appear in every section: QQQ spot 605, strike 545, $45,000 premium, September 2026 expiry; Meta $640 cost basis, $760 strike, $2,850 premium; NVIDIA $180 at 40% vol → $4.54 one-day sigma; SEBI 2024 study finding 89% of F&O traders net losers at ₹1.1 lakh average loss.

Four 'Well, actually 🧐' sidenotes appear across Sections II-V, flagging basis risk in ETF hedging, Black-Scholes' violated assumptions (constant vol, no jumps, log-normal tails), theta's mathematical acceleration near expiry, and log-normal vs normal distribution asymmetry.

The claim is reducible to two sentences: 'A put option caps your portfolio loss at premium plus the gap to strike — that is your insurance deductible.
